pattern: deep crawl as decorator on arun(), split-brain cache (SQLite+FS), freshness via HTTP+head fingerprint, browser version recycling
provider-aware aggregation, normalization in __setattr__, vqd bootstrap pattern
learnings-as-state recursion, breadth halving, all LLM calls schema-constrained

Fireplexity’s most distinctive pattern is its use of AI SDK `data-*` message parts as a parallel stream for sources, status, tickers, and follow-up prompts alongside token streaming. It also couples retrieval-time finance intent detection with inline TradingView embedding, which makes the search-answer flow behave more like a domain-aware research surface than a plain chat wrapper.

this project mixes a standard single-agent research pipeline with two separate multi-agent orchestration stacks, while also supporting MCP as a two-stage tool-selection-plus-tool-execution retrieval path. It is also unusual in coupling report generation, persisted report history, and follow-up chat over the same research artifact.

MindSearch’s distinctive pattern is `generate_graph_code` plus `execute_planner_code`: the planner does not just emit subqueries, it emits executable Python that mutates a live search graph. Its other standout trait is `stream_node_progress` + `stash_node_snapshots`, exposing planner/searcher internals as first-class UI state instead of hiding them behind a final answer.

This project is unusually explicit about action selection: the agent must choose among `search`/`visit`/`reflect`/`answer`/`coding` under a strict schema, then survive a rejection loop with evaluator feedback before finalizing. It also separates evidence grounding from answer drafting by doing post-hoc late-chunk retrieval, citation matching, and footnote injection after the main answer is produced.

This repo contains two separate decomposition styles in code: a newer supervisor plus parallel-researcher LangGraph and an older section-planning workflow plus legacy multi-agent supervisor. It also unusually bakes MCP auth/token exchange, native model web-search detection, and benchmark/evaluation pipelines directly into the codebase rather than treating them as external glue.

OpenManus’s distinctive pattern is that the same ReAct loop can swap between local tools, MCP-federated remote tools, and Daytona/Docker sandbox tools with minimal agent changes. It also feeds live browser state and screenshots back into the next-step prompt, so browser interaction is prompt-conditioned rather than just action-conditioned.

This project’s distinctive pattern is a lightweight live-search stack that fuses heterogeneous evidence types in one context window: organic snippets, scraped page text, top stories, knowledge graph data, and answer-box content. Its `pro_mode` is also unusual because it changes retrieval behavior end to end at once: fewer search hits, targeted page crawling, semantic chunking, and reranking before streamed answer generation.

Scira’s most distinctive pattern is the way it fuses mode-specific tool packs, resumable SSE chat streams, and subscription-aware gating into one search runtime. The other standout is its blend of “agentic research” outputs: extreme-search can move from multistep retrieval to code execution to canvas-style visual reporting, while the same app also exposes MCP apps, scheduled lookouts, and voice search.

The project’s most distinctive pattern is its lightweight multiplexed stream protocol: raw sources first, then answer text, then related questions, separated by explicit markers the UI incrementally parses. It also couples that stream with KV-backed replay keyed by `search_uuid`, making generated results both shareable and cacheable without rerunning search plus generation.

The project’s most distinctive pattern is its sentinel-based streaming protocol, where one response interleaves serialized sources, answer text, and related questions using `__LLM_RESPONSE__` and `__RELATED_QUESTIONS__` markers. It also combines static-exported Next.js assets and the Sanic API into one deployable Python service, which is unusually compact for an AI-search stack.

SearXNG’s distinctive pattern is its runtime-loaded engine federation: one shared search pipeline adapts to hundreds of engine modules via traits, processor types, and hookable result normalization. It also couples that federation with first-class public-instance defenses, so anti-abuse, proxying, and observability are part of the core product rather than bolt-ons.

Swirl’s most distinctive pattern is its DB-configured query-transform fallback, where processor names can resolve either to Python classes or user-managed `QueryTransform` records at runtime. It also uses a two-pass relevancy design that pushes provider-side feedback into post-result rescoring, then layers RAG page fetching with summary fallback on top of the federated result set.

Vane’s most distinctive pattern is its block-based answer stream: research plans, search steps, sources, widgets, and final prose are all emitted as separate live artifacts instead of one opaque completion. It also unifies local semantic file search with web, academic, and discussion search inside the same LLM-driven planner, so uploaded documents behave like another first-class search backend.
